data.title: Coca Cola Bottlers Japan Holdings Q1 Losses Keep Profit Turnaround Narratives Under Scrutiny
data.description:
Advertisement

Setting the scene with Q1 2026 numbers

Coca-Cola Bottlers Japan Holdings (TSE:2579) opened 2026 with Q1 revenue of ¥196.5 billion and a basic EPS loss of ¥5.59, alongside a net loss of ¥922 million, after a stretch of mixed quarterly results. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from ¥189.8 billion in Q1 2025 to ¥228.2 billion in Q2, ¥265.6 billion in Q3, and ¥210.3 billion in Q4. Basic EPS shifted from a loss of ¥36.67 in Q1 2025 to a profit of ¥101.42 in Q3, before returning to losses of ¥12.15 in Q4 and ¥5.59 in Q1 2026. This sets up an earnings print where investors may pay close attention to how efficiently revenue converts into profit. Margins remain under pressure, so the story around cost discipline and profitability will likely be front of mind as you assess these results.

See our full analysis for Coca-Cola Bottlers Japan Holdings.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the prevailing narratives, and where the data challenges what the market has been assuming about Coca-Cola Bottlers Japan Holdings.

See what the community is saying about Coca-Cola Bottlers Japan Holdings

Losses still heavy on a ¥900.6b revenue base

  • On a trailing 12 month basis, Coca-Cola Bottlers Japan Holdings generated ¥900.6b of revenue but recorded a net loss of ¥45.2b and a basic EPS loss of ¥268.59, so the business is still loss making despite the large top line.
  • Consensus narrative highlights plans to lift profit margins from about a 5.7% loss today to 3.5% profit within three years. However, the current trailing loss and modest 0.5% annual revenue growth show that the margin improvement story is starting from a weak base.
    • Analysts are looking for earnings to move from a loss of ¥50.8b to profit of ¥32.1b by around 2029, while the latest trailing numbers still show losses in the tens of billions of yen.
    • This gap between current loss levels and the profit targets is what readers should focus on when weighing whether the margin and earnings goals look realistic against the recent track record.

Valuation looks cheap against revenue and DCF

  • The shares trade at ¥3,254, compared with a DCF fair value estimate of ¥4,854.70 and a P/S of 0.6x versus about 1x for peers and 2.2x for the wider Asian beverage group, so the stock is priced lower than those reference points while the company is still unprofitable.
  • Bullish investors point to this P/S discount and the gap to DCF fair value as support for a value case, yet the five year pattern of losses worsening at about 30.5% per year on average means the valuation signals sit alongside a tough earnings history.
    • Revenue on a trailing 12 month basis has inched up only 0.5% per year compared with a 5.9% growth rate for the broader Japan market, which helps explain why some investors question how quickly the valuation gap could close.
    • The existence of a 2.21% dividend yield alongside these losses also matters, because the dividend has not been well covered by earnings over the last year even though some bulls point to shareholder returns as part of the appeal.
On these numbers, some readers will want to see how optimistic investors connect the dots between today’s loss making profile and their value argument for the stock, which you can do by checking the 🐂 Coca-Cola Bottlers Japan Holdings Bull Case.

Forecast turnaround vs recent loss trend

  • Over the last year, revenue grew about 0.5% while the company stayed in the red, and losses over the past five years have increased at roughly 30.5% per year, even as forecasts in the data suggest earnings could grow around 53.11% per year and move into profit within three years.
  • Bears focus on this contrast, arguing that a market with modest revenue growth and a history of widening losses makes those high growth and profit turnaround forecasts demanding, especially when the broader Japan market is growing faster at 5.9% per year.
    • The trailing 12 month loss of ¥45.2b and basic EPS loss of ¥268.59 underline that the turnaround has not yet shown up in the reported figures, which is central to the cautious view.
    • At the same time, the current share price of ¥3,254 sits below the analyst consensus price target of ¥4,025.00, so anyone taking the bearish angle needs to be comfortable questioning both the growth assumptions and that target level.
If you are weighing this cautious angle, it helps to see how skeptics frame the risks around pricing, vending volumes and long term goals in more detail by reviewing the 🐻 Coca-Cola Bottlers Japan Holdings Bear Case.

data.title: Teladoc Health Q1 Loss Reduction Reinforces Bullish Margin Narrative Despite Flat Revenue
data.description:
Teladoc Health (TDOC) opened 2026 with Q1 revenue of US$613.8 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.36, alongside a trailing twelve month EPS loss of US$0.96 on revenue of US$2.5 billion. Over recent quarters, the company has seen revenue move from US$629.4 million in Q1 2025 to US$613.8 million in Q1 2026, while quarterly EPS shifted from a loss of US$0.53 to a loss of US$0.36 as net income moved from a loss of US$93.0 million to a loss of US$63.8 million. This keeps the focus firmly on how quickly margins can tighten and losses can shrink from here.

See our full analysis for Teladoc Health.

With the latest results on the table, the next step is to set these margin trends against the most common Teladoc Health narratives to see which stories the numbers support and which they call into question.

See what the community is saying about Teladoc Health

Advertisement

Losses easing on a trailing basis

  • On a trailing 12 month view, net loss narrowed from US$200.3 million at Q4 2025 to US$171.1 million at Q1 2026, and basic EPS loss improved from US$1.14 to US$0.96 over the same period.
  • Supporters of the bullish view point to this pattern of shrinking losses, arguing it lines up with the longer term 32.2% annual loss reduction and the idea that Teladoc can move toward better margins even while revenue growth sits at 1.7% a year.
    • That said, quarterly net loss in Q1 2026 at US$63.8 million is wider than Q4 2025 at US$25.1 million, which challenges a straight line bullish story and shows the path has been uneven.
    • The bullish narrative expects profit margins to eventually turn positive, but the trailing numbers still show an unprofitable business, so the data partly supports and partly questions how quickly that margin shift can happen.

Recent loss trends and the mixed quarterly pattern are exactly what bullish investors debate when they argue Teladoc is on the cusp of a more profitable phase, so if that angle interests you it is worth seeing how those arguments are laid out in full 🐂 Teladoc Health Bull Case

Flat revenue trend with modest 1.7% growth

  • Reported revenue over the last six quarters has moved in a fairly tight band between US$613.8 million and US$642.3 million, which lines up with the 1.7% annual growth rate on a trailing 12 month basis and sits well below the cited 11% wider US market growth rate.
  • Bears focus on this modest top line pace, arguing that a roughly flat revenue run rate combined with ongoing losses makes it harder for Teladoc to offset rising compliance, data privacy, and reimbursement costs that are highlighted in the cautious narrative.
    • The Q1 2026 revenue figure of US$613.8 million is lower than Q4 2025 at US$642.3 million, which fits with the bearish concern that growth could stall if competition and reimbursement pressures bite.
    • At the same time, revenue has not collapsed, and the trailing 12 month total of about US$2.5b shows the business still has scale, so the data challenges the idea that the addressable opportunity is already capped.

For readers weighing whether these revenue patterns back up the more cautious arguments about Teladoc's business mix and competitive position, it can help to see how skeptics frame the risks in more detail 🐻 Teladoc Health Bear Case

Low 0.4x P/S against DCF fair value

  • Teladoc trades on a P/S of 0.4x versus peer and industry averages of 2.2x and 2.1x, and the stated DCF fair value of US$11.68 sits well above the current share price of US$6.06, even though analysts expect the company to remain unprofitable over the next three years.
  • Supportive investors highlight this valuation gap, arguing that the combination of a 48.1% discount to DCF fair value and loss reduction over five years makes the current multiple look conservative, while more cautious voices question how long the market will wait for profitability when revenue growth is running at just 1.7% a year.
    • The consensus analyst price target of US$7.10 is only modestly above the current share price, which contrasts with the much larger gap to the DCF fair value and shows that not all valuation frameworks are pointing to the same level of upside.
    • The P/S discount to peers comes alongside continued losses of US$171.1 million on a trailing 12 month basis, so the low multiple is linked directly to the unprofitable status rather than clear evidence of mispricing.

data.title: Tokyo Electron (TSE:8035) Margin Improvement Tests Bullish Earnings Growth Narratives
data.description:
Tokyo Electron (TSE:8035) closed out FY 2026 with a strong final quarter, reporting Q4 revenue of ¥711.8 billion and basic EPS of ¥469.22, while trailing twelve month revenue stood at ¥2.44 trillion and EPS at ¥1,254.57. Over the past six reported quarters, revenue has ranged from ¥549.6 billion to ¥711.8 billion and quarterly EPS from ¥257.13 to ¥469.22, giving investors a clear view of how the top and bottom line have moved through the cycle. With trailing net profit margin at 23.5% versus 22.4% a year earlier, the latest numbers indicate that profitability is holding up well.

See our full analysis for Tokyo Electron.

With the headline figures on the table, the next step is to see how these results line up against the key Tokyo Electron narratives investors have been trading on, and where the data starts to push back on them.

See what the community is saying about Tokyo Electron

Advertisement

TTM earnings growth sits at 5.6% with margins at 23.5%

  • Over the last 12 months, earnings grew 5.6% and trailing net profit margin stands at 23.5%, compared with 22.4% a year earlier, on trailing revenue of ¥2.44b and net income of about ¥574.5b.
  • Consensus narrative suggests long term demand for advanced chip equipment and services can support higher margins, and the current 23.5% margin partly lines up with that. At the same time, the 5.6% earnings growth is more modest than the roughly 10% per year average over five years and sits below the roughly 13.9% earnings growth that is forecast, which sets a clear hurdle for the bullish view.
    • Supporters of the bullish narrative point to accelerating AI server demand and an expanding field solutions business. Both of these are expected to lift earnings faster than the 5.6% growth just reported.
    • The step up in margin from 22.4% to 23.5% is also consistent with the argument that ongoing R&D and capacity investments can support higher profitability even when reported growth is not yet at forecast levels.
On the bullish side, some investors think the current 23.5% margin is only a starting point for Tokyo Electron as AI driven equipment demand ramps and new facilities scale up. This is the kind of setup they use to build a more optimistic long term story about the company. 🐂 Tokyo Electron Bull Case

Valuation signals mixed with 37.4x P/E and DCF gap

  • The shares trade on a 37.4x P/E with a current price of ¥47,620, compared with an industry average P/E of 27.5x, a cited peer group average of 44.6x, and a DCF fair value of ¥25,298.73, while a single allowed analyst price target reference is ¥48,580.95.
  • Bears argue that a richer P/E than the Japan semiconductor industry and a share price almost twice the DCF fair value could limit upside, and the current numbers give that view some support. However, the P/E is below the peer average and the allowed analyst target of ¥48,580.95 sits only slightly above the current ¥47,620 level, which suggests expectations embedded in that single reference point are not extreme.
    • The gap between the ¥47,620 share price and the ¥25,298.73 DCF fair value is one of the clearest data points bears highlight when they argue the market is paying a high multiple for the existing earnings base.
    • On the other hand, the 37.4x P/E being below the 44.6x peer average leaves room for investors who focus on relative valuation rather than the DCF reference to argue the stock is not the most expensive name in its group.

Share price volatility contrasts with steady multi year earnings record

  • Over the past three months the share price has been relatively volatile compared with the Japan market, while earnings have grown about 10% per year on average over the last five years and 5.6% over the past year according to the provided summary.
  • Critics highlight that this near term volatility, combined with heavy exposure to China at about 38.6% of quarterly sales and dependence on a handful of large customers, means the recent earnings record and margin profile could be tested if customer capex delays persist. This is the case even though the current trailing margin of 23.5% and the multi year earnings growth history indicate the business has, so far, been able to support a relatively high earnings base through cycles.
    • Bears focus on the risk that further slowdowns in equipment orders or tighter export rules could make that earnings growth path choppier than the five year average suggests, which would matter given the elevated P/E.
    • At the same time, the fact that earnings still grew 5.6% over the last year during a period that includes customer investment pauses stands against the idea that short term volatility in the share price necessarily reflects a break in the fundamental record.
Skeptical investors often point to that recent volatility, alongside the concentration in China and key customers, as a reason to stress test the cautious case more closely before leaning on the five year earnings track record. 🐻 Tokyo Electron Bear Case

data.title: ResMed Shifts Leadership And Adds Neurology With Noctrix Acquisition
data.description:
• ResMed (NYSE:RMD) appointed Aaron Bloomer as its new Chief Financial Officer, following the retirement announcement of long-serving CFO Brett Sandercock.
  • The company also agreed to acquire Noctrix Health, a medical device firm focused on treatments for restless leg syndrome.
  • Both announcements highlight changes in financial leadership and an expansion of ResMed’s portfolio beyond its core sleep apnea business.

ResMed is best known for its sleep apnea devices and related software, a segment that sits at the intersection of respiratory care and digital health. The addition of Noctrix Health brings exposure to restless leg syndrome, an adjacent neurological condition that also affects sleep quality. For investors, that broadens the clinical scope of NYSE:RMD beyond its traditional sleep apnea focus.

The arrival of Aaron Bloomer as CFO, together with the Noctrix acquisition, gives you new data points to watch around capital allocation, acquisitions and product mix. As these moves are implemented, the key questions will be how ResMed balances investment in new neurology related devices with its existing sleep apnea and software offerings, and how that combination shapes the company’s long term direction.

data.title: Thomson Reuters Capital Return Plan Advances And Reshapes Investor Outlook
data.description:
• Thomson Reuters received court approval for its return of capital and share consolidation plan after a shareholder vote at a special meeting.
  • The Ontario Superior Court of Justice approved the plan of arrangement, which includes a return of capital to shareholders and a reverse stock split.
  • The plan is pending final approvals from stock exchanges and is expected to become effective soon.

For investors watching TSX:TRI, this court approval confirms that a previously announced corporate action is moving closer to execution. Thomson Reuters shares last closed at CA$129.94, with the stock up 5.2% over the past week and 3.5% over the past month, but showing a 25.2% decline year to date and a 48.5% decline over the past year. Over a longer horizon, the shares show a 20.6% gain over five years, with returns over three years at a 15.6% decline.

The approved plan will directly affect existing holders through the return of capital and a change in share count once the consolidation takes effect. Investors may want to watch for final exchange approvals and any further company updates on timing, ratio details, and how the return of capital will be paid, to assess what this action means for their own position in TSX:TRI.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Thomson Reuters by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Thomson Reuters.

📰 Beyond the headline: 2 risks and 2 things going right for Thomson Reuters that every investor should see.

This return of capital and share consolidation plan sits squarely in Thomson Reuters' broader capital allocation approach, where management has also used buybacks and a growing pipeline of AI-powered products to reshape the business. A special cash distribution returns funds to shareholders, while the reverse stock split reduces the number of shares and raises the trading price per share without changing the company’s total equity value. For you as an investor, the economic impact will depend on tax treatment of the capital return in your market and how your broker handles fractional shares following the consolidation. The transactions are also a signal about how management currently views excess cash versus reinvestment or acquisitions. With the plan expected to be effective on May 4, 2026, final approval from the Toronto Stock Exchange and Nasdaq is a key short term milestone, along with any further detail on the consolidation ratio and timing of the cash payout.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Thomson Reuters Narrative

  • The capital return and consolidation align with a story of cash generation that can fund AI-powered workflow tools while still returning surplus cash to shareholders.
  • If large cash distributions reduce flexibility for future M&A or AI investments, that could affect expectations for product expansion in legal and tax solutions.
  • The specific effects of this plan on share count and per share metrics, such as earnings per share, may not yet be fully reflected in existing narrative assumptions.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Thomson Reuters to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Execution risk around the reverse stock split, including how fractional shares are treated and whether liquidity changes after the consolidation.
  • ⚠️ A large cash distribution may reduce balance sheet flexibility if operating conditions or acquisition opportunities change.
  • 🎁 Returning capital in cash can be attractive if you prefer immediate distributions over the company holding excess cash.
  • 🎁 The consolidation could maintain or improve index eligibility and comparability with peers such as RELX, Wolters Kluwer, and Bloomberg’s privately held operations, which some investors use as reference points.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, focus on the final terms of the capital return and consolidation, including the effective date, the consolidation ratio, and the treatment of fractional shares. Watch how trading volumes and bid ask spreads behave after the reverse split, as this can affect how easily you can enter or exit a position. It is also worth tracking future commentary from management on how this action fits with ongoing AI product rollouts and any future buyback activity, as that will show how capital returns balance against reinvestment priorities.

7.9% Net Margin Sets The Tone

  • Over the last 12 months, Nippon Densetsu Kogyo converted ¥229,207 million of revenue into ¥18,060 million of net income, which works out to a 7.9% net margin compared with 6.1% a year earlier and 36.9% earnings growth over that period.
  • Bulls point to this 7.9% margin and 36.9% earnings growth as evidence of a solid profit engine, yet the same data shows earnings growth over the next three years is forecast at around a 0.4% decline each year, which heavily tests that bullish stance.

data.description:
ZOZO (TSE:3092) has wrapped up FY 2026 with fourth quarter revenue of ¥56,568 million and basic EPS of ¥12.38, while trailing twelve month EPS sits at ¥54.11 on revenue of ¥228,373 million as earnings grew 5.7% over the last year. TTM earnings growth holds at 5.7%

  • On a trailing basis, net income is ¥47,926 million on revenue of ¥228,373 million, which lines up with 5.7% earnings growth over the last year and roughly 8.4% average annual earnings growth over five years.
  • Consensus narrative sees this steady earnings expansion as backed by factors such as more active users and AI driven services. However, the modest step down in net margin from 21.3% to 21.0% shows that, while growth is present, it is being balanced against extra costs for advertising, promotions and integrating new services. This keeps profitability essentially steady rather than sharply improving.

21% net margin faces cost and competition risks

  • Trailing net profit margin is 21.0% versus 21.3% a year ago, with quarterly net income over FY 2026 ranging from ¥9,630 million to ¥15,970 million on revenue between ¥51,221 million and ¥66,556 million.
  • Bears argue that heavy reliance on Japan, rising logistics and promotion expenses, and pressure from global rivals could squeeze these margins over time. The data so far shows margins holding close to 21%, so the bearish concern is more about how long ZOZO can keep funding initiatives such as free shipping campaigns and Lyst integration without a clearer margin lift.
    • For example, the bearish narrative highlights higher promotion to GMV ratios and potential ESG related cost pressure, while the current 21.0% margin is only modestly below last year and not a sharp break in profitability.
    • The range of quarterly net income in FY 2026, topping out at ¥15,970 million in Q3, also shows that earnings remain meaningful even as these cost items rise.
Bears who see margin risk as the key issue may want to check how the cautious case stacks up against ZOZO's actual profit track record so far 🐻 ZOZO Bear Case

  • Bullish investors point to that DCF fair value being about 11.6% above the market price and to forecast earnings growth of around 6.8% a year. At the same time, the premium P/E versus peers means the bullish case leans on continued execution on factors such as AI personalization and the Lyst tie up to justify paying more than the sector multiple for what is currently mid single digit growth and a slightly lower margin than last year.

data.title: McGrath RentCorp (MGRC) Margin Compression Challenges Bullish Rental Profitability Narratives
data.description:
McGrath RentCorp (MGRC) opened 2026 with Q1 revenue of about US$198.5 million and basic EPS of roughly US$1.10, while trailing twelve month revenue stood at US$947.4 million with EPS of about US$6.30. The company reported quarterly revenue of US$195.4 million and EPS of roughly US$1.15 in Q1 2025, followed by a peak of US$256.8 million in revenue and EPS of about US$2.02 in Q4 2025 before this latest quarter. This progression provides context for how investors may assess margins as the new year begins.

See our full analysis for McGrath RentCorp.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings print lines up with the widely followed narratives about McGrath RentCorp and where those stories get tested by the data.

See what the community is saying about McGrath RentCorp

Advertisement

Margins Compress as Net Profit Slips to 16.4%

  • Trailing 12 month net income of US$155.1 million on US$947.4 million of revenue works out to a 16.4% net margin, compared with 25.8% a year earlier on US$910.9 million of revenue and US$231.7 million of net income.
  • Consensus narrative points to recurring rental demand as a support for profitability, yet the drop in margin from 25.8% to 16.4% suggests cost pressure and segment softness are having a clear impact even while trailing revenue has risen from US$910.9 million to US$947.4 million.
    • Critics highlight weaker segment demand and higher operating costs in areas like Mobile Modular and Portable Storage, which lines up with lower margins despite trailing earnings per share of US$6.30 still being described as high quality.
    • Supporters who focus on long term rental adoption need to reconcile that positive theme with the recent compression in net margin, since that shift directly affects how much of each rental dollar converts to profit.

Share Price, P/E and Valuation Gap Stand Out

  • At a share price of US$110.55 and trailing EPS of about US$6.30, McGrath RentCorp trades on a P/E of 17.5x, below both the peer average of 27.3x and the US Trade Distributors industry at 23.4x, while a DCF fair value of US$138.15 and an analyst price target of US$144.60 both sit above the current price.
  • Consensus narrative talks up recurring rental revenue and long term growth potential, and the current valuation gap tests that view by suggesting the market is pricing the recent slowdown in forecast growth at 3.4% for revenue and 6.6% for earnings more cautiously than analysts who see about 30.8% upside to targets.
    • What stands out is that five year earnings grew at about 18.8% a year, yet forward growth forecasts are now in the mid single digits, which may explain why investors are not assigning peer level P/E multiples despite the premium implied by the DCF fair value.
    • At the same time, the 1.79% dividend yield and lower P/E versus peers give some support to the view that investors who accept slower forecast growth are not paying the same multiple as for faster growing trade distributors.

Quarterly EPS Trend and Balance Sheet Trade Off

  • Quarterly basic EPS moved from US$1.15 in Q1 2025 to US$2.02 in Q4 2025 and then to about US$1.10 in Q1 2026, while trailing EPS over the last four quarters sits at roughly US$6.30 and the company continues to carry a high level of debt alongside its 1.79% dividend yield.
  • Bears argue that softer demand in some rental segments, higher selling and general administrative expenses and reliance on existing fleet inventory, combined with that high debt load, could pressure both earnings and cash generation, and the recent margin decline and EPS pattern across the last four quarters give some support to that cautious view even as rental demand and diversification efforts underpin the longer term story.
    • Weaker utilization readings in areas such as Portable Storage and Mobile Modular have been highlighted as pressure points, which lines up with the move in trailing net margin from 25.8% to 16.4% on broadly similar sub US$1b revenue levels.
    • At the same time, the reliable dividend and history of five year earnings growth around 18.8% per year show why some investors still see room for returns if leverage is managed and segment demand stabilizes.
Skeptics focus on the margin compression and debt load, while others point to the valuation gap and rental model strengths, so it can be useful to see how the full bear case frames these same numbers before deciding how they fit your own view of the business.🐻 McGrath RentCorp Bear Case

Approach 1: Veradermics Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to what they might be worth in today’s dollars. It is essentially asking what a stream of future cash flows is worth right now.

For Veradermics, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $71.63 million. Analyst and model projections then move through a period of negative free cash flows, such as $69 million in 2026 and $95.50 million in 2027, before reaching a projected free cash flow of $123 million in 2030. Beyond the analyst window, Simply Wall St extrapolates cash flows out to 2035 using its own growth assumptions.

Combining all of these discounted cash flows results in an estimated intrinsic value of about $250.72 per share. Against a current share price around $99, the DCF output implies the stock trades at roughly a 60.5% discount to this estimate, which indicates a wide gap between price and modeled value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Veradermics Price vs Book

For companies in pharmaceuticals, price to book, or P/B, can be a useful yardstick because it compares what you pay in the market with the accounting value of net assets on the balance sheet. For profitable, established businesses, investors often look for a P/B that reflects both the quality of those assets and the earnings they can support.

Growth expectations and risk also shape what feels like a normal P/B level. Faster growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher uncertainty can point to a lower one. This is why simple comparisons can be tricky.

Veradermics currently trades on a P/B of 30.09x, which sits well above the Pharmaceuticals industry average of 2.63x and the peer group average of 4.33x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what P/B might make sense given Veradermics’ earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risks. Because it pulls these company specific factors together, the Fair Ratio can be a more tailored benchmark than broad industry or peer averages.

In this case, Veradermics’ actual P/B of 30.09x is meaningfully higher than the Fair Ratio. This points to the shares screening as expensive on this measure.

Narratives are available on Simply Wall St’s Community page, used by millions of investors, and they update automatically when new information such as news, filings or earnings is added. This helps ensure your view does not stay frozen while the facts move.
